_note: The Pharaohs of Egypt — a SEPARATE Egyptian tradition, deliberately NOT connected to Adam's line. This is a king-list rendered as a SUCCESSION chain, not a strict father-son bloodline: Egyptian kingship passed across five dynasties (Dyn 1 through Dyn 12) broken repeatedly by the collapse of the Old Kingdom and the First Intermediate Period, so no single documented bloodline runs the length of it. Where a real father-son link exists it is noted in the label (Sneferu->Khufu, Khufu->Djedefre->Khafre, Pepi I->Pepi II line). All figures here are HISTORICAL — attested by contemporary monuments and king-lists (Palermo Stone, Turin Canon, Abydos list); only the identification of Narmer with the legendary unifier 'Menes' is uncertain, and that is flagged. Dates are conventional 'middle chronology' estimates (Met Museum / Britannica) and scholars disagree by decades before c.700 BC. Dates strictly DESCEND down the chain. All ids prefixed EG_ so they classify to the EGYPT house. This is its own root; it has no bloodline link to the rest of the tree by design.

a.label:
The Pharaohs of Egypt
c.3200 BC · the succession of Egyptian kingship (a king-list, not a strict father-son bloodline)
· a separate tradition with no link to Adam's line

children.label:
Narmer (Menes?)
c.3100 BC · 1st Dynasty; unified Upper & Lower Egypt — the Narmer Palette shows him in the white and red crowns · historical (identification with legendary 'Menes' uncertain)

Hor-Aha
c.3080 BC · 1st Dynasty; successor (likely son) of Narmer; early Memphis and Abydos tombs · historical

Den
c.2970 BC · 1st Dynasty; first king shown wearing the Double Crown of the Two Lands · historical

Khasekhemwy
c.2690 BC · last king of the 2nd Dynasty; reunified Egypt after internal conflict; father of the 3rd Dynasty · historical

Djoser
c.2670 BC · 3rd Dynasty; his architect Imhotep built the Step Pyramid at Saqqara — the first monumental stone building · historical

Sneferu
c.2600 BC · founder of the 4th Dynasty; built three pyramids (Meidum, Bent, Red) — perfected the true smooth-sided pyramid · historical

Khufu (Cheops)
c.2560 BC · 4th Dynasty; son of Sneferu; built the Great Pyramid of Giza, the last surviving Wonder of the Ancient World · historical

Djedefre
c.2528 BC · 4th Dynasty; son of Khufu; first king to take the title 'Son of Ra' · historical

Khafre (Chephren)
c.2520 BC · 4th Dynasty; son of Khufu (brother of Djedefre); built the second Giza pyramid and, most likely, the Great Sphinx · historical

Menkaure (Mycerinus)
c.2490 BC · 4th Dynasty; son of Khafre; built the smallest of the three Giza pyramids · historical

Userkaf
c.2465 BC · founder of the 5th Dynasty; began the tradition of building sun temples to Ra · historical

Sahure
c.2458 BC · 5th Dynasty; mounted the first recorded seafaring expedition to Punt; richly decorated pyramid at Abusir · historical

Unas
c.2350 BC · last king of the 5th Dynasty; his pyramid bears the earliest Pyramid Texts — the oldest religious writings on Earth · historical

Teti
c.2323 BC · founder of the 6th Dynasty; Manetho records he was murdered by his own bodyguard · historical

Pepi I Meryre
c.2289 BC · 6th Dynasty; long, prosperous reign; survived a harem conspiracy against him · historical

Merenre I
c.2255 BC · 6th Dynasty; son of Pepi I; pushed Egyptian trade and control south into Nubia · historical

Pepi II Neferkare
c.2246 BC · 6th Dynasty; son of Pepi I; reputedly reigned ~90 years — the longest of any monarch in recorded history; his reign ended in the slow collapse of the Old Kingdom · historical

First Intermediate Period
c.2160 BC · central kingship collapses; Egypt splinters into rival dynasties at Herakleopolis and Thebes (Dyn 7–10) — a break in the succession, not a single king

Mentuhotep II
c.2040 BC · 11th Dynasty; reunified Egypt by conquest and founded the Middle Kingdom; ruled from Thebes · historical

Amenemhat I
c.1991 BC · founder of the 12th Dynasty; a vizier who took the throne and built a new capital, Itjtawy; likely assassinated · historical
